description: Developmental support fund enabling Metro Denver nonprofits to build new skills and grow organizational capacity. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Capacity Building. Geographic scope: Regional (7-county Metro Denver, CO). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits in Metro Denver seeking developmental support for organizational growth. Award: varies (developmental support; includes coaching, learning, and financial assistance). Deadline: rolling/annual (check denverfoundation.org/funding-opportunities).

title: The Denver Foundation — Bowen Family Performing Arts Fund
description: Helps children from low-income Denver families access dance, acting, and singing programs. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Arts & Culture, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (Metro Denver, CO). Eligibility: Nonprofits providing performing arts opportunities (dance, theater, singing) for children from low-income families in Metro Denver. Award: varies (named endowed fund). Deadline: annual (check denverfoundation.org/funding-opportunities for cycle dates).

title: The Denver Foundation — Lowe Fund (Intellectual & Developmental Disabilities)
description: Named donor fund supporting Colorado nonprofits focused on intellectual and developmental disabilities. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Healthcare (intellectual/developmental disabilities). Geographic scope: Regional (Colorado statewide). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits in Colorado supporting training, education, and research into causes, cures, and treatment of intellectual and developmental disabilities. Award: $10,000–$100,000. Deadline: annual (March annually; 2026 deadline was March 16; check denverfoundation.org for 2027 date).

title: Triangle Community Foundation — Responsive Grantmaking Program
description: Relaunched in spring 2025 to address federal funding instability; funds organizational capacity and core service maintenance. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Capacity Building, Community Development. Geographic scope: Regional (Triangle, NC: Wake, Durham, Orange, Chatham counties). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits based in and serving one or more Triangle counties; 3+ years of service and impact. Award: up to $20,000 per grant ($693,675 awarded to 174 nonprofits in FY2025–2026). Deadline: Rolling — continues on rolling basis while funding available (check trianglecf.org/award/fund-for-the-triangle-responsive-grantmaking or contact nonprofitservices@trianglecf.org for current status).

title: Triangle Community Foundation — Education & Youth Grant
description: Two-year general operating support for Triangle nonprofits advancing youth-led initiatives and educational completion rates. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Education, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (Triangle, NC). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) youth-serving nonprofits based in and serving Triangle counties; must serve youth ages 5–24. Award: $40,000 over 2 years ($20,000/year general operating support). Deadline: annual (biannual cycles; check trianglecf.org for next open cycle).

title: Triangle Community Foundation — Triangle Capacity-Building Network (TCBN) Grant
description: Shifts power and capital to BIPOC-led nonprofits in the Triangle; bilingual Spanish RFP available. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Capacity Building. Geographic scope: Regional (Triangle, NC). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits or fiscally sponsored orgs led by and serving people of color; 85% of funding to orgs with budget under $1M. Award: varies (participatory grantmaking process). Deadline: annual (check trianglecf.org for next open window; participatory process).

title: Triangle Community Foundation — Housing Affordability Grant
description: New flagship grant program launched in 2025 addressing Triangle’s housing crisis. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Community Development (affordable housing). Geographic scope: Regional (Triangle, NC). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits addressing housing affordability and homelessness in the Triangle region. Award: ~$25,000 per grantee (inaugural cycle: $375,000 to 15 nonprofits). Deadline: annual (inaugural 2025 cycle complete; 2026 cycle expected spring 2026; check trianglecf.org).

title: Target Circle Community Giving
description: Target Circle members vote to direct a portion of Target’s community giving to registered nonprofits. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: Education, Arts & Culture, Community Development. Geographic scope: National (store-level). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits in the US; apply to be a Target Circle nonprofit partner via target.com/circle/community-giving. Award: donations driven by Target Circle member votes (typically 1%–5% of store sales allocated to community partners per vote share). Deadline: rolling (nonprofits register as partners; voting cycles occur at local store level).

title: Target Foundation — Strategic Grantmaking
description: Target’s primary foundation vehicle for large-scale strategic investments. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: Education, Community Development, Economic Opportunity. Geographic scope: National (priority: Minneapolis-St. Paul, MN). Eligibility: Nonprofits in Target’s priority markets; primarily invitation-based. Award: $500,000–$5,000,000+ (multi-year strategic partnerships; $50M+ annually). Deadline: invitation-based (check corporate.target.com/sustainability-esg for any announced open calls).

title: Lowe’s Hometowns Community Grant
description: Combines cash, materials, and employee volunteer labor for community improvement projects; 2,000+ projects since 2022. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: Community Development, Capacity Building. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits, public schools, and units of government with community improvement projects; must be near a Lowe’s store; project must involve Lowe’s volunteer team. Award: up to $25,000 in materials + Lowe’s volunteer labor (part of $100M Hometowns initiative over 5 years). Deadline: rolling (check lowes.com/l/about/community-impact for current application portal).

title: Lowe’s Foundation — Gable Grants (Skilled Trades Education)
description: Lowe’s Foundation’s flagship workforce grant; one of the largest corporate workforce investment programs in the US. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education (skilled trades). Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits and community/technical colleges offering skilled trades training programs (carpentry, electrical, plumbing, HVAC, welding). Award: $500,000–$2,000,000 per award (part of $50M Gable Grants commitment over 5 years; 80+ organizations funded; 50,000+ workers trained). Deadline: annual (check lowesfoundation.org/gable-grants for 2026–2027 cycle dates; new awards expected fall 2026).

title: Coastal Bend Community Foundation — Competitive Grants (Corpus Christi area)
description: Serves 10 South Texas counties anchored by Corpus Christi. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Education, Arts & Culture, Healthcare, Community Development, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (Coastal Bend Texas: Nueces, San Patricio, Aransas, Kleberg, Jim Wells, Bee, Refugio, Brooks, Live Oak, Duval counties). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits and units of government serving the Coastal Bend region of South Texas. Award: varies (multiple named funds; annual competitive cycles). Deadline: annual (check coastalbendcf.org/grants for current cycle).

title: Heart of Texas Community Foundation — Grant Programs (Bryan/College Station)
description: Serves 7-county Brazos Valley region centered on Bryan/College Station; $1.4M+ granted in FY2024. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Education, Healthcare, Arts & Culture, Community Development, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (Brazos Valley, TX: Brazos, Burleson, Leon, Madison, Milam, Robertson, Washington counties). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits serving the Brazos Valley region of Texas. Award: varies (HOTCF awarded $1.4M+ in grants in FY2024). Deadline: annual (check hotcf.org/grantmaking for current cycle; spring and fall application windows historically).

title: Toshiba America Foundation — STEM Grants for Grades 6–12 (Under $5,000)
description: Direct-to-teacher STEM grants; $79,852 awarded to 20 projects Sept. 2025; $105,302 to 23 projects Feb. 2025. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: 6th–12th grade teachers in US public or nonprofit private schools; project-based STEM learning with measurable outcomes; one grant per school at a time. Award: up to $5,000. Deadline: Rolling — year-round (grants under $5,000 accepted on rolling basis; apply via toshiba.com/taf/612-2 anytime).

title: Toshiba America Foundation — STEM Grants for Grades 6–12 (Over $5,000)
description: Larger-scope classroom STEM projects; recent grantees included water quality testing and watershed analysis projects. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: 6th–12th grade teachers in US public or nonprofit private schools. Award: $5,001–$25,000+ ($194,250 awarded to 43 projects in Feb. 2026 cycle). Deadline: biannual (Feb. and Sept./Oct. submission windows; next window expected September/October 2026 — check toshiba.com/taf for exact dates).

title: Toshiba America Foundation — STEM Grants for Grades K–5
description: Annual elementary-level STEM grant; funds hands-on science and math learning experiences for K–5 students. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: K–5 classroom teachers in US public or nonprofit private schools; innovative, inquiry-based science and math projects. Award: up to $1,000. Deadline: annual (October 1, 2026 — confirmed on toshiba.com/taf/k-5).

title: Seattle Foundation — Neighbor to Neighbor (N2N) Grants
description: Seattle Foundation’s signature community grantmaking program; quarterly grants to grassroots King County nonprofits. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Community Development, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (King County, WA). Eligibility: Nonprofits providing programmatic and professional assistance to low-income and racial minority community members in King County. Award: varies (quarterly grants; $63M+ awarded to 2,000+ organizations historically). Deadline: quarterly (summer 2026 cycle paused for planning; fall 2026 cycle expected to reopen — check seattlefoundation.org/current-grant-opportunities for reopening date).

title: Seattle Foundation — First Peoples Climate Fund
description: Landmark $5.5M investment advancing Tribal sovereignty and Indigenous leadership in climate resilience; co-funded by City of Seattle. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Environment, Community Development. Geographic scope: Regional (Washington State / Pacific Northwest). Eligibility: Tribal nations and Indigenous-led nonprofits in Washington State working on climate sovereignty and land stewardship. Award: varies ($5.5M total investment; 7 grantees announced April 14, 2026). Deadline: fixed date (inaugural cycle announced April 14, 2026; check seattlefoundation.org for future cycles).
funder: Seattle Foundation (partnership with City of Seattle Office of Sustainability & Environment)

title: Seattle Foundation — BIPOC Nonprofit Effectiveness Grants
description: Builds resilience and capacity of BIPOC-led King County nonprofits; part of Seattle Foundation’s Blueprint for Impact and REPAIR initiative. Funder type: community foundation. Focus areas: Capacity Building. Geographic scope: Regional (King County, WA). Eligibility: BIPOC-led nonprofits in King County with deep experience providing programs sustaining Black, Indigenous, and People of Color communities. Award: varies (part of REPAIR initiative; $25M committed to Black communities through 2026). Deadline: rolling/invitation-based (check seattlefoundation.org/nonprofits for current competitive grant cycles; contact grants@seattlefoundation.org or 206.515.2109).

title: Robert R. McCormick Foundation — Thriving Communities: Englewood & Little Village
description: One of Chicago’s largest foundations ($1.75B+ assets; $1.8B+ granted since 1955). Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Community Development, Youth Programs, Education. Geographic scope: Regional (Chicago South and West Sides: Englewood and Little Village). Eligibility: Chicago-based nonprofits working in Englewood or Little Village with deep local knowledge; invitation-only. Award: $30,000–$10,000,000 ($78.2M in annual grants across all programs). Deadline: invitation-only (contact info@mccormickfoundation.org; 312-445-5000).

title: Robert R. McCormick Foundation — Early Childhood Education Grants
description: Funds programs giving children greater opportunity to succeed; includes access to quality early learning, family literacy, and school readiness. Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Education, Youth Programs. Geographic scope: Regional (Chicago / Illinois). Eligibility: Illinois nonprofits, schools, and early childhood organizations; invitation-only. Award: $30,000–$10,000,000 ($78.2M annual grantmaking). Deadline: invitation-only.

title: Robert R. McCormick Foundation — Journalism & Democracy Grants
description: Funds investigative reporting, ethnic media, community news, and journalism training. Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Arts & Culture (journalism/media), Community Development. Geographic scope: Regional (Chicago/Illinois; some national journalism partnerships). Eligibility: Local and regional nonprofit news organizations, civic media orgs, and journalism training programs; invitation-only. Award: $30,000–$10,000,000 (part of $78.2M annual grantmaking). Deadline: invitation-only.

title: Robert R. McCormick Foundation — Communities Fund (United Way Partnership)
description: Crowd-funded giving with Foundation match; partners with WGN-TV, Chicago Cubs, Chicago Bulls, Chicago White Sox, Chicago Blackhawks, and Northern Trust. Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Community Development. Geographic scope: Regional (Chicago neighborhoods + 4 suburbs). Eligibility: Nonprofits in United Way Neighborhood Network communities (9 Chicago neighborhoods + Berwyn, Cicero, Elgin, Waukegan); via United Way of Metro Chicago partnership. Award: varies (50-cents-on-the-dollar Foundation match; 100% of donations plus match go to nonprofits). Deadline: rolling/campaign-based (partnered campaigns with United Way; check mccormickfoundation.org/partnerships).

title: NSF Advancing Informal STEM Learning (AISL)
description: NSF’s primary grant for informal STEM learning at museums, libraries, science centers, nature centers, and community orgs; $60M+ per year. Funder type: government. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: US colleges/universities, nonprofits, museums, science centers, libraries, community organizations, and informal learning environments. Award: $300,000–$3,000,000 (varies by project type). Deadline: annual (2026 deadline: September 18, 2026 — confirmed on nsf.gov; check nsf.gov for 2027 deadline).

title: NSF Improving Undergraduate STEM Education (IUSE: EHR)
description: NSF’s largest undergraduate STEM education grant; funds curriculum innovation, faculty development, active learning, and broadening participation in STEM. Funder type: government. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Education. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: US colleges, universities, community colleges, nonprofits partnering with higher education on undergraduate STEM. Award: $300,000–$3,000,000 (varies by track). Deadline: annual (multiple windows; 2026 target dates: January 15 and July 15 — check nsf.gov for 2027 solicitation).

title: NSF Civic Innovation Challenge (CIVIC)
description: Community-driven research grants solving real local problems via university-community partnerships; funded 200+ projects since 2020. Funder type: government. Focus areas: STEM & Technology, Community Development, Environment. Geographic scope: National. Eligibility: US colleges/universities and nonprofit research orgs partnering with local governments, tribes, or community organizations. Award: Stage 1: $25,000–$50,000; Stage 2: $750,000–$1,000,000 (per team). Deadline: annual (Stage 1 deadline typically January; Stage 2 typically September; check nsf.gov for FY2027 solicitation).
funder: National Science Foundation (with U.S. Department of Energy)

title: Weyerhaeuser Company Foundation — Environment & Community Grants
description: Timber and forest products company foundation; strong environment, forestry education, and community development focus in timber-dependent communities across 11+ states. Funder type: corporate. Focus areas: Environment, Community Development, Education. Geographic scope: Regional (communities where Weyerhaeuser operates: WA, OR, AL, AR, GA, LA, MS, NC, OK, TN, TX, WI). Eligibility: 501(c)(3) nonprofits and schools in Weyerhaeuser operating communities. Award: $1,000–$50,000 (community grants); $3.8M+ in annual giving. Deadline: rolling (apply via weyerhaeuser.com/sustainability/communities).

title: Surdna Foundation — Thriving Cultures Grants (Arts & Culture)
description: New York-based Straus family foundation funding arts and culture as drivers of social change. Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Arts & Culture, Community Development. Geographic scope: National (priority: historically underrepresented communities). Eligibility: US 501(c)(3) nonprofits; primarily invitation-based; submit LOI via surdna.org; $145M+ in assets. Award: $50,000–$500,000 (multi-year general operating support typical; $21M+ annually). Deadline: invitation-based (check surdna.org/our-programs for any open calls; contact grants@surdna.org).

title: Charles Stewart Mott Foundation — Civil Society Program
description: Flint, MI-based foundation ($3.7B assets; $9B+ granted since 1926); funds civil society organizations, philanthropy infrastructure, and nonprofit sector defense. Funder type: foundation. Focus areas: Community Development, Capacity Building. Geographic scope: National (US and international). Eligibility: US 501(c)(3) nonprofits working on civic engagement, philanthropy infrastructure, nonprofit sector strengthening, and access to justice; no unsolicited proposals as of January 2025.
